coredata.dc:title: A novel thioctic acid-carbon dots fluorescence sensor for the detection of Hg2+ and thiophanate methyl via S-Hg affinity
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shuo
Chen, Hengye
Xie, Hongliang
Wei, Liuna
Xu, Lu
Zhang, Lei
Lan, Wei
Zhou, Chunsong
She, Yuanbin
Fu, Haiyan
coredata.dc:description: Mercury ions and thiophanate methyl (TM), are common contaminants present in the environment and food products. These contaminants cause neurovirulence and carcinogenicity effect on the human body. Herein, thioctic acid-carbon dots (SCDs) was synthesized and applied in a fluorescent “turn-off–on” probe to detect Hg2+ and TM. The presence of other common metal ions and pesticides did not affect the response of the developed sensor. Further investigation revealed that the fluorescent “turn-off–on” model were static, wherein the “turn-off” was induced by an electron transfer effect, while the “turn-on” was caused by the formation of TM-Hg complexes. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence sensor method exhibited limits of detection as low as 33.3 nmol/L and 7.6 nmol/L for Hg2+ and TM, respectively. The developed sensor was designed to detect Hg2+ and TM in real tap water, grape juice and Citri Reticulatae Pericarpium (CRP) water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thioctic acid-carbon dots
Mercury ions
Thiophanate-methyl
Fluorescence sensor
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ and TM in real samples
Sensing mechanisms of SCDs for Hg2+ and TM
Conditions optimization of sensing for Hg2+ and TM
Selectivity of SCDs fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ and TM
Establishment of sensing model for Hg2+ and TM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High resolution S2p XPS spectrum of SCDs (A), SCDs + Hg2+ (B) and SCDs/Hg2+ + TM (C).

Schematic illustration of the detection of Hg2+ and TM by SCDs sensing model (A). The visualization results before (B, C) and after centrifugation (D, E). SCDs (tube 1), SCDs + Hg2+ (tube 2), SCDs + TM (tube 3), SCDs/Hg2+ + TM (tube 4), Hg2+ + TM (tube 5). Tube 1 to 5 are displayed from left to right. The centrifugation operation is 6000 r/min, 5 min. The wavelength of UV equipment is 365 nm. The above mentioned concentration of Hg2+ and TM are both 10 μmol/L.

Fluorescent lifetime of SCDs (black line), SCDs + Hg2+ (red line) and SCDs/Hg2+ + TM (blue line). τ1, τ2 and τavg were fast, slow and average fluorescence lifetime (ns). A1 and A2 were the weight factor of τ1 and τ2, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The HRTEM image and the particle size distribution map (A), XRD and SAED results (B), and 3-D fluorescence spectra (C, D) of SCDs.

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence spectra of SCDs reacted with different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.05–5.8 μmol/L). Inset is a linear regression diagram between F1/F0 and concentrations of Hg2+ (A). Fluorescence spectra of the SCDs/Hg2+ system in the presence of TM with different concentrations (0.05–5.0 μmol/L). Inset is a linear regression diagram between F2/F1 and concentrations of TM (B).
Comparison of the proposed work with previous literature reported Hg2+ and TM detection methods.

head.ce:title: A novel thioctic acid-carbon dots fluorescence sensor for the detection of Hg2+ and thiophanate methyl via S-Hg affinity

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Shuo Wang and Hengye Chen equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury ions and thiophanate methyl (TM), are common contaminants present in the environment and food products. These contaminants cause neurovirulence and carcinogenicity effect on the human body. Herein, thioctic acid-carbon dots (SCDs) was synthesized and applied in a fluorescent “turn-off–on” probe to detect Hg2+ and TM. The presence of other common metal ions and pesticides did not affect the response of the developed sensor. Further investigation revealed that the fluorescent “turn-off–on” model were static, wherein the “turn-off” was induced by an electron transfer effect, while the “turn-on” was caused by the formation of TM-Hg complexes. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence sensor method exhibited limits of detection as low as 33.3 nmol/L and 7.6 nmol/L for Hg2+ and TM, respectively. The developed sensor was designed to detect Hg2+ and TM in real tap water, grape juice and Citri Reticulatae Pericarpium (CRP) water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the rapid industrialization and economic development, considerable attention has been directed toward heavy metal and pesticide pollution. In particular, the formation of mercury (Hg) and thiophanate-methyl (TM) residues should be extensively investigated because these contaminants severely affect the human health and environment (Chowdhury, Mazumder, Al-Attas, & Husain, 2016; Li, Sun, Pu, & Jayas, 2017). In addition, there are serious concerns regarding Hg and TM contamination in the food industry, resulting in an overall loss of credibility and economic frustration. Hg contamination can arise from natural and industrial sources such as volcanic eruptions, fossil fuels, and mining, and the Hg contaminants can spread through the water, air, soil, and food products (Yang, Peng, Han, Song, & Wang, 2020). Exposure to Hg largely occurs via the consumption of Hg-contaminated agricultural, aquatic, and other food products, causing a variety of reproductive, physiological and biochemical abnormalities (Guo, Kang, Liang, & Zhang, 2020). Hg may exist in its elemental state, or as organic/ inorganic salts in the environment. Amongst all states, Hg2+ and its derivatives are particularly non-biodegradable and persistent, leading to the contamination of water, soil, and food products (Raicopol et al., 2020). In addition, Hg2+ can easily penetrate the skin or enter through the respiratory tract, leading to the irreversible disruption of immune, endocrine, and central nervous systems, thereby causing various health problems (Kim, Kabir, & Jahan, 2016).

Pesticide contamination in soil, water, and atmosphere can occur in various ways, and it impacts the food industry and environmental safety (Jia et al., 2020). TM is a highly effective broad-spectrum benzimidazole fungicide with low toxicity. TM residues can be detected in the soil and agricultural products, such as vegetables and fruits. TM and carbendazim (methyl benzimidazol-2-ylcarbamate) can be interconverted and both contaminants persist in crops for long durations, resulting in excessive pesticide residues in agricultural products. This poses a serious threat to human health as TM and carbendazim contamination can cause endocrine disruption, embryotoxic, and teratogenic effects (Jiang et al., 2017). Therefore, the development of a rapid, sensitive and selective method for the detection of Hg2+ and TM is essential for environmental protection and human health safety.

To date, numerous techniques, such as graphite furnace atomic absorption spectroscopy (GF-AAS) (Duarte et al., 2016), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Habte et al., 2016), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) (Xu et al., 2015) and electrochemical methods (Roy, Prasad, Tevatia, & Saraf, 2018) have been applied to quantitatively monitor and control the Hg2+ concentration in various samples. Moreover, most studies on the detection of TM focus on the use of liquid chromatograph-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Dong, Yang, Pang, & Hu, 2018), ultraviolet absorption spectroscopy (Zheng et al., 2018), surface-enhanced Raman imaging (Li, Sun, Pu, & Jayas, 2017) and other sophisticated methods. Although these methods are sensitive and precise, they are also complicated, time-consuming, expensive and difficult to operate. Furthermore, these methods require sophisticated instrumentation and involve tedious sample preparation, making these techniques inappropriate for rapid on-site application. Thus, it is imperative to develop a simple, rapid and low-cost method that can be applied for on-site Hg2+ and TM detection. Compared with some conventional methods, sensors based on fluorescent measurements have garnered considerable attention for heavy metals and pesticides detection because these sensors are sensitive and time-efficient. (Liu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Venkateswarlu et al., 2020). Quantum dots (QDs), upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs), and mesoporous silica NPs (MSNPs) have been applied as fluorescent sensors to detect Hg2+ and pesticides (Ullah, Mansha, Khan, & Qurashi, 2018; Wang et al., 2019). However, the requirement of expensive raw materials, complex synthetic steps and the use of toxic chemicals limit the widespread application of these methods. Therefore, there is a need indispensable to synthesize a safe, simple, and environment friendly nanomaterial with good fluorescent properties.

Carbon dots (CDs) are considered as promising candidates for fluorescent probes because they are eco-friendly and process good biocompatibility, low toxicity, unique optical properties, and excellent water solubility. In addition, the synthesis of CDs is cost-effective (Arul & Sethuraman, 2018). However, the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield of CDs are generally low (Siddique, Singh, Pramanick, & Ray, 2020). Previous studies have suggested that the fluorescence properties of CDs can be enhanced by coordinating functional groups to the CD surface. Thus, considerable efforts have been devoted to increasing their fluorescence properties by doping the CDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus and boron, etc. (Guo, Cao, & Li, 2018; Li, Feng, & Ma, 2020).

In an attempt to develop a safe, sensitive and specific fluorescent probe with a good detection range, herein, sulfur-doped CDs (SCDs) were successfully synthesized using dl-thioctic acid as the sulfur source. dl-thioctic acid has been previously used as a metal scavenger because of its strong chelation with metal ions (Bjørklund, Aaseth, Crisponi, Rahman, & Chirumbolo, 2019). Further, the prepared SCDs were designed to accurately detect Hg2+ and TM through a novel fluorescent “turn-off–on” model, which has not been reported in previous studies. The prepared SCDs emitted blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light with a quantum yield higher than that of CDs (without thioctic acid), along with good aqueous solubility and photostability. The fluorescence of the SCDs was selectively quenched in the presence of Hg2+ (turn-off) and the quenched fluorescence was recovered by the addition of TM (turn-off–on). Additionally, the possible mechanisms for Hg2+ and TM detection were elucidated. Under optimal experimental conditions, the developed SCD-based sensor exhibited satisfactory detection limits and concentration ranges for Hg2+ and TM detection. More importantly, the results of this study suggest that the reported fluorescence “turn-off–on” approach with high selectivity and sensitivity can be applied to detect Hg2+ and TM in the environment and agro-products.

Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Wuhan, China) was the supplier of dl-thioctic acid (98%), trisodium citrate dihydrate, sodium hydroxide, DMF and a variety of metal salts (HgAc2, AgNO3, CuSO4, CdCl2, Pb(NO3)2, SnCl2, MnAc2, MgCl2, NiNO3, ZnAc2, CoAc2, AlCl3, BaCl2, FeCl3, KCl and NaCl) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Common pesticides, viz., (thiophanate methyl, paraquat, bensulfuron methyl, tebufenozide, dipterex, carbendazim, quinclorac, lambda cyhalothrin, beta cypermethrin, carbaryl, carbofuran, fenvalerate, monosultap, cartap, deltamethrin and isoprocorb) were purchased from Shanghai Pesticide Research Institute Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All experiments used deionized distilled water (DDW), which was obtained from the Molgeneral 220a water purification system (Molecular). National Resource Center for Chinese Materia Medica, China Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences (Beijing, China) was the main supplier of Citri Reticulatae Pericarpium (CRP). Fresh grapes were purchased from the local market (Wuhan, China).

All fluorescence spectra were recorded on the F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and an excitation wavelength (λex) of 340 nm was used. The UV spectra were recorded on a UV-1800PC spectrophotometer (Mapada, Shanghai, China). Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700-FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, USA) was used to acquire the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN instrument (FEI, Chicago, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Multilab 2000 instrument (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes (FLTs) were evaluated on a FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (EI, Edinburgh, Britain).

The water-soluble SCDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal synthesis (Gupta et al., 2015). Briefly, dl-thioctic acid (0.2 g) was dissolved in DMF (10 mL) via sonication. This solution was added to a solution of trisodium citrate dihydrate (1.0 g) and NaOH (10 mg) in DDW (30 mL). The resulting solution was sonicated for 2 min. After complete dissolution, the mixed solution was heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove impurities and filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to yield a clear yellow SCDs solution. CDs were synthesized by a similar procedure, wherein dl-thioctic acid was not used.

All experiments were performed at room temperature and pressure. The SCDs solution (100 μL) was diluted by adding PBS buffer solution (700 μL) and DDW (100 μL). Hg2+ solutions (100 μL) of different concentrations were added such that the total volume of the resulting solution was 1 mL. The solutions were kept undisturbed for 5 min and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The fluorescence intensity was quenched in different degrees (turn-off). The excitation wavelength, voltage, and slit widths (λex, λem) were 340 nm, 400 V and 10 nm, respectively.

The standard procedure and parameter settings for detecting TM were similar to those mentioned in section 2.4. Briefly, the SCDs solutions (100 μL) were mixed with PBS buffer (700 μL) and Hg2+ solutions (100 μL) (turn-off). TM solutions (100 μL) of different concentrations were added to the mixed solutions. The solutions were kept undisturbed for 5 min and the fluorescence spectra were measured. The fluorescence intensity was recovered to different degrees (turn-on).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ and TM in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water was filtered to remove all large impurities through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The CRP was made by dipping in DDW (100 °C) for 15 min and then filtered to get CRP water. The grape juice sample was prepared by diluting freshly pressed grape juice. The Hg2+ and TM solutions were prepared by real tap water, grape juice, and CRP water samples without any further processing. The procedure for detection and instrument parameters were the same as those mentioned in section 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanisms of SCDs for Hg2+ and TM
ce:section.ce:para: In the detection of Hg2+, non-fluorescent SCD-Hg complexes are formed, as indicated by the peak corresponding to the Hg-S bond at 161.90 eV, which appeared after the addition of Hg2+ (Fig. 1B). In addition, after adding Hg2+, a new FLT (τ2) appeared and it was much smaller than τ1 (red line in Fig. 3); thus, changing the fitting function from single exponential decay for SCDs solution only to double exponential decay for the solution containing SCDs and Hg2+. This change in the FLT fitting function indicated that electron transitions occurred from the conduction band to the surface defect of SCDs, leading to differences in the surface passivation of SCDs (Wang et al., 2019) and easier electron transfer reactions between the SCDs and Hg2+, thereby quenching the fluorescence. Moreover, the average FLT (τavg) of SCDs was unaffected by the addition of Hg2+, indicating that the fluorescence turn-off is static and nonfluorescent complexes (SCDs-Hg) were formed. Previous reports have suggested the occurrence of electron transfer reactions in the CDs-Hg2+ system (Liu et al., 2013) and numerous studies have proposed that the electron transfer can be facilitated by doping the CDs with S atoms (Li et al., 2014).

After the addition of TM, dark-yellow nonfluorescent TM-Hg complexes were formed (Fig. 2B–E), as indicated by the peak corresponding the an Hg-S bond at 161.90 eV, which was observed throughout the experiment (Fig. 1C). In addition, τ2 disappeared after the addition of TM, and the FLT curve fitted well with the single exponential decay function (blue line in Fig. 3), indicating that the electron transitions occurred from the conduction band and valence band, and there were no electron transfer reactions between the SCDs and Hg2+, thereby restoring the fluorescence of SCDs. Moreover, the τavg value remained unchanged, indicating that the fluorescence turn-on is static and nonfluorescent complexes (TM-Hg) were formed.

The morphology, crystallinity, and fluorescence characteristic of the SCDs were examined by HRTEM (Fig. 4 A), XRD and the corresponding SAED (Fig. 4B), and 3-D fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4C, D). For HRTEM analysis, the synthesized SCDs were dispersed in ethanol and uniformly distributed over a carbon-coated Cu grid. The diameters of the SCDs ranged from 2.21 nm to 3.01 nm, and the mean diameter was 2.52 nm. The apparent lattice spacing was 0.22 nm, which can be attributed to the typical (100) facets of graphite (Li et al., 2020). The XRD results of the SCDs displayed a diffused peak centered at 2θ = 28.4°, which corresponds to the (002) diffraction patterns of the graphitic carbon (Arul & Sethuraman, 2018). In addition, the diffused ring pattern observed in the SAED for SCDs suggested that the presence of amorphous carbon (Arul & Sethuraman, 2018). The 3-D fluorescence spectra of SCDs showed that the optimal excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) wavelengths were 340 nm and 438 nm, respectively. And the Em peak did not change with change in Ex, indicating that the SCDs surface was relatively uniform and well-passivated (Zhai et al., 2014).

The FT-IR spectra of SCDs was shown in Fig. S1 and exhibited characteristic peaks of SCDs. A broad absorption peak at 3424 cm−1 (OH stretching) and a strong peak centered at 1596 cm−1 (CO stretching) indicated the existence of oxygen and carbon-containing functional groups. The characteristic peaks at 2930 cm−1 and 1414 cm−1 can be attributed to the CH stretching of a –CH3 group. The peaks at 2329 cm−1 and 1151 cm−1 correspond to the SH and CS stretching frequencies, respectively, indicating that sulfur from dl-thioctic acid was successfully doped on the carbon dots.

The XPS spectrum of the prepared SCDs showed six characteristic peaks centered at 163.3, 228.0, 284.7, 400.1, 497.0, and 531.1 eV, which were observed in the S 2p, S 1s, C 1s, N 1s, Na KLL, and O 1s XPS spectra, respectively (Fig. S2). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2B) presented their different peaks at 284.72, 286.01 and 288.18 eV in SCDs which could be contributed to the CC/CC, CO and CO bonds, respectively (Huang et al., 2013; Jia, Li, & Wang, 2012). The high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2C) showed different peaks at 531.04, 531.90 and 535.53 eV that can be attributed to the CO, CO, and OC–O bonds in SCDs, respectively (Gupta et al., 2015). The presence of different kinds of carbon and oxygen bonds indicated the existence of different C and O states on the SCDs surface. In addition, the two peaks at 163.30 and 164.47 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of S 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. S2D) correspond to the CS 2p3/2 and CS 2p1/2 bonds (Mu, Hua, Feng, & Yang, 2019), indicating that the dl-thioctic was successfully coordinated to the carbon dots and provided a sufficient amount of sulfur to assist in the detection of Hg2+ with high specificity.

The FLT curves of the SCDs are shown in Fig. 3. The τ1 and τavg values of SCDs were both equal to 7.30 ns and the FLT curves fitted with the single exponential decay function, indicating that electron transitions occurred from the conduction band to the valence band in SCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conditions optimization of sensing for Hg2+ and TM
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the F1/F0 and F2/F1 ratios (F0, F1, and F2 correspond to the fluorescence intensity of SCDs, SCDs + Hg2+, SCDs/Hg2+ + TM solutions, respectively) were studied. Hg2+ and TM solutions (3 μmol/L) were used, the pH of the system was adjusted by adding PBS buffer (0.1 mol/L), and the mixed solution was incubated for 5 min. As displayed in Fig. S3A, B, the F1/F0 ratio increased with increasing pH with a maximum at pH = 8.0; however, further increase in pH caused the F1/F0 to decrease. In contrast, the F2/F1 values decreased with increasing pH with a minimum at pH = 8.0. Further increase in pH (pH > 8.0) caused the F2/F1 ratio to increase. Therefore, further investigations were conducted at a solution pH of 8.0.

Then, the effect of response time on the detection of Hg2+ and TM also studied. Hg2+ and TM solutions (3 μmol/L) were used and PBS buffer (0.1 mol/L) was added to maintain a pH of 8.0. In Fig. S3C, D, the value of F1/F0 and F2/F1 stabilized after 5 min, manifesting that the reactions (SCDs + Hg2+ and SCDs/ Hg2+ + TM) reached completion within 5 min. Hence, 5 min was chosen as the optimal response time in the following experiments.

As the key parameter affecting TM detection, the concentration of Hg2+ directly affects the limit of detection (LOD) and detection range of TM. In order to achieve a sensitive detection with practical significance, we optimized the concentration of Hg2+ in the process of TM detection. As per the current Chinese standard “GB2763-2019”, the maximum residual limit for TM (3.0 mg/kg or 8.8 μmol/L) is much higher than other international limits (Lee et al., 2018). Thus, an optimal concentration of Hg2+ in the SCDs/Hg2+ system was selected such that the detection system was responsive to a lower TM concentration (1.5 μmol/L). As shown in Fig. S3F, when the concentration of TM was 1.5 μmol/L, the value of F2/F1 increased with an increase in Hg2+ concentration, and a maximum was observed at 5.5 μmol/L. Further increase in the Hg2+ concentration caused the F2/F1 values to decrease. Consequently, the Hg2+ concentration was fixed at 5.5 μmol/L in the process of TM detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of SCDs fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ and TM
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of SCDs on various common metal ions (including Hg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Ag+, K+, Na+) were studied. As shown in Fig. S4A, the detection of Hg2+ by the SCDs was largely unaffected by the presence of other metal ions, which could be due to the surface passivation of SCDs facilitates the binding of Hg2+. In this step, the pH value of the PBS buffer was 8.0, and the incubation time was 5 min. The concentration of Hg2+ and other metal ions were 5 μmol/L and 100 μmol/L, respectively.

While evaluating the selectivity of the SCDs/ Hg2+ system toward TM, it is important to consider the effects of other compounds, such as sulfur-containing pesticides, TM derivatives, sulfur-containing active compounds, and other common pesticides. Thus, the selectivity of the SCDs/ Hg2+ system toward TM was investigated in the presence of thiophanate methyl, paraquat, bensulfuron methyl, tebufenozide, dipterex, carbendazim, quinclorac, lambda cyhalothrin, beta cypermethrin, carbaryl, carbofuran, fenvalerate, monosultap, cartap, deltamethrin, and isoprocorb. As shown in Fig. S4B, the fluorescence intensity of the SCDs/Hg2+ system was similar to that of the SCDs only in the presence of TM. All other pesticides did not react with the SCDs/ Hg2+ system, possibly because of the strong affinity between Hg2+ and the two mercapto groups of TM, resulting in the formation of TM-Hg complexes. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of the SCDs is restored only in the presence of TM. In this step, the pH value of the PBS buffer was 8.0, and the incubation time was 5 min. The concentration of Hg2+, TM, and other pesticides were 5.5 μmol/L, 5 μmol/L, and 100 μmol/L, respectively.

Besides, as shown in Fig. S5, as reference material, the fluorescence of the CDs (without dl-thioctic acid in preparation) was not restored after the addition of TM in the CDs/Hg2+ system and the fluorescence yield of the CDs was much lower than that of SCDs, indicating that SCDs synthesized by dl-thioctic acid is crucial in the sensing model. The effect of the highly active sulfhydryl compound, l-cysteine (100 μmol/L), on the detection of TM was also investigated. Contrary to our expectations, l-cysteine exhibited a weak response toward TM and was easily oxidized to cystine by air in neutral and weakly alkaline solutions. Thus, l-cysteine was not used in the detecting system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of sensing model for Hg2+ and TM
ce:section.ce:para: A standard curve for Hg2+ detection was constructed by measuring the fluorescence of the SCDs solution containing gradient concentration of Hg2+ at the optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 A, after the addition of Hg2+, the emission peak of the SCDs solution at 438 nm was strongly quenched without any red shift or blue shifts, revealing that the Hg2+ solution can efficiently quench the fluorescence of SCDs. The fluorescence intensity of SCDs at 438 nm gradually decreased as the Hg2+ concentration increased with a minimum intensity at 5.8 μmol/L. The F1/F0 values was plotted against the concentration of Hg2+ to obtain the linearity of the SCDs solution for Hg2+ detection. The calibration curve between the F1/F0 values and the Hg2+ concentration in a range from 0.05 to 5.8 μmol/L presents an eminent linear relationship between the two parameters with a correlation coefficient of 0.9996 (inset of Fig. 5A). The linear regression equation was fitted with the Stern-Volmer equation, and it can be expressed as F1/F0 = −0.1483[CHg] + 0.9802. The LOD (3σ/KSV, n = 3) was calculated as 33.3 nmol/L.

Similarly, a calibration curve for TM detection was also constructed by adding TM solutions with varied concentrations into the SCDs/Hg2+ system. As shown in Fig. 5B, with the increase of TM from 0.05 to 5.0 μmol/L, the quenched fluorescence intensity of the SCDs/Hg2+ system at 438 nm gradually increased to the original level. Fig. 5B displays two calibration curves; a plot between the F2/F1 values and the concentration of TM in the range from 0.05 to 2.0 μmol/L and another similar plot in a TM concentration range of 2.0 to 5.0 μmol/L. Both curves present good linear relationships with correlation coefficients of 0.9998 and 0.9983, respectively. And the linear regression equations can be expressed as F2/F1 = 0.6027[CTM] + 1.0630 and F2/F1 = 0.2684[CTM] + 1.7285, respectively. The LOD (3σ/KSV, n = 3) was 7.6 nmol/L.

As presented in Table 1 , the linearity and LODs of the proposed sensing models for Hg2+ and TM detection were compared to those previously reported in literature studies. It is apparent that the proposed model has more satisfactory results in detection ranges and LODs, and makes some progress based on other works.

Although the sensor based on SCDs was originally developed for the detection of Hg2+, it’s was discovered that TM, even at low concentrations, can efficiently combine with Hg2+, modifying the sensor to a fluorescent “turn-off–on” model. After developing such a fluorescence turn-off–on method based on a strong S-Hg bond, we found that TM can also be detected through the generation of TM-Hg complexes, which provided a new idea for the detection of TM. In the following work, we will focus on developing a new method to simultaneously detect and remove heavy metals or pesticides.

The results of this study demonstrate that the SCD-based fluorescent probe for Hg2+ and TM detection has numerous advantages, such as excellent sensitivity, preeminent selectivity, rapid response, and simple operation. Thus, the developed probe was applied for Hg2+ and TM detection in real samples. Briefly, the tap water was filtered to remove all large impurities through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The CRP was dipped DDW (100 °C) for 15 min and then filtered to get CRP water. The fresh grapes were pressed to afford grape juice, which was centrifuged (8000 r/min, 5 min) and the supernatant was filtered to remove all large impurities with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The resulting sample was diluted 5 times before further investigation. All real samples were used as solvents to prepare the Hg2+ and TM solutions without any further processing. All tests were repeated 3 times and the results were listed in Tables S1 and S2. The recovery of the Hg2+ ranged from 96.67% to 105.75% and relative standard deviation (RSD) was in the range of 0.22%-5.46%. Besides, the recovery of the TM ranged from 96.28% to 110.23% and the RSD were in the range of 0.41%–2.76%. The response of the SCDs-based fluorescence sensor was unaffected by the complicated interference matrices in tap water, CRP water, and grape juice. According to literature research, the co-existence of Hg2+ and TM in fruits has not yet been reported before. But the co-existence of heavy metals and pesticides in the soil has been reported, and these contaminants will eventually find their way into the aquatic environment (Wallace and Buha Djordjevic, 2020). Thus, if both Hg2+ and TM were present in tap water, insoluble Hg-TM complexes would have been formed and these complexes were removed during filtration. In lake water, insoluble impurities and large particles eventually settle in the silt. Consequently, the applicability of the developed fluorescent sensor was tested in tap water, grape juice, and CRP water samples and the detection of Hg2+ and TM was unaffected by their possible co-existence in the tested samples. The recovery results obtained in this study demonstrated that the proposed sensor has a broad application prospect for the rapid and selective detection of Hg2+ and TM in different real complex real samples.

In summary, SCDs were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal approach involving the carbonization of dl-thioctic and trisodium citrate dihydrate. The relatively uniform and well-passivated SCDs with a mean particle diameter of 2.52 nm displayed a higher quantum yield and better fluorescence properties than those of CDs. Additionally, the prepared SCDs were applied as a highly selective, sensitive, and effective sensor for Hg2+ and TM detection. The detection mechanism depended on the formation of SCDs-Hg2+ complexes and electron transfer reaction between SCDs and Hg2+, leading to the static fluorescence quenching of SCDs. The addition of TM removed Hg2+ from the SCDs/Hg2+ system, forming visible TM-Hg complexes, and thus, restoring the fluorescence intensity of SCDs. Furthermore, the SCDs-based sensor was designed to detect Hg2+ and TM in tap water, grape juice, and CRP water samples with satisfactory results. These findings demonstrate the broad application prospect of SCDs in monitoring trace amounts of contaminants in the environment and the quality control of food safety products.

Shuo Wang: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Hengye Chen: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Hongliang Xie: Investigation. Liuna Wei: Formal analysis. Lu Xu: Formal analysis. Lei Zhang: Formal analysis. Wei Lan: Resources. Chunsong Zhou: Resources. Yuanbin She: Funding acquisition, Resources. Haiyan Fu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the financial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2020YFC1712700), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants nos. 31972164, 21776321, 32001789, 21665022, 21776259), Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Department (Nos. QKHJC [2017]1186, QKHZC [2019]2816, and QKHPTRC [2020]5009), the Talented Researcher Program from Guizhou Provincial Department of Education (QJHKYZ [2018]073), and Tongren Science and Technology Bureau (No.TSKY2019-3), the Talented Youth Cultivation Program from “the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities”, and South-Central University for Nationalities (No. CZP20007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.128923.

ce:section.ce:para:
